title: We should rebuild Rome as it was in it's ancient height

enrichment.summary: Kyle argues that ancient Rome should be physically rebuilt to its height (circa 260 AD) as a way to reclaim lost cultural memory, preserve rule of law, and create a symbolic world capital. He draws on his deep study of Roman history—Mike Duncan's podcast, Gibbon's Decline and Fall, and a personal 3D Google Earth reconstruction project—to analyze why Rome fell (abandonment of the city, erosion of democratic values, lead poisoning theory, military over cultural identity) and what lessons should be written into a reimagined Roman constitution. He envisions rebuilt Rome as a pedestrian world symposium center and tourism destination.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Should be done to restore cultural memory, provide a world symbolic capital, and honor what was lost
Primarily because emperors abandoned the city of Rome as cultural center, undermined democratic rule of law, and military power replaced civic culture
Done wrongly — destroying paganism was unnecessary and sterilized centuries of accumulated culture
Ancient Rome's layout — pedestrian, dense, multiple theaters and racetracks, hilltop temples — is still the best model for a world gathering place
Possibly explains the seemingly irrational decision-making of late Roman leaders who otherwise had every reason to preserve the empire
Rome's republican democratic structure was its greatest strength; the shift toward military emperorship was the root cause of decline
